description:
You are a configuration-manager who has been handed a small archive of historical application configuration files.  
All files are already present on the system under:

/home/user/configs/

    ├── config_20230115.conf
    ├── config_20230410.conf
    └── config_20230718.conf

Each file is an ordinary UTF-8 text file that follows the pattern “key = value”, but:
 • Lines may start or end with arbitrary horizontal whitespace.  
 • Comment lines start with “#” (possibly preceded by whitespace) and must be ignored when counting or extracting keys.  
 • Blank lines are possible and must likewise be ignored.  
 • Keys are case-insensitive; treat them as lower-case for every report.  

Your job is to produce a set of summary artefacts using awk and/or sed (shell redirection and coreutils are also allowed).  
You should **not** rely on Python, Perl, jq, or other scripting languages—just standard UNIX text-processing utilities.

When you are finished, four new filesystem objects must exist and have the exact, testable contents/structure described below.

1. Directory: /home/user/output/  
   (Create it if it does not exist.  Any reasonable user permissions such as 755 are fine.)

2. Directory: /home/user/clean_configs/  
   (Create it if it does not exist.  Same permission guidance as above.)

3. File: /home/user/output/config_line_counts.tsv  
   Format requirements: tab-separated values, **no header**, Unix LF line endings.  
   Each line must contain:
       a) the original filename, exactly as it appears in /home/user/configs/  
       b) a tab character (\t)  
       c) the count of “active” (i.e. non-blank, non-comment) lines in that file.  
   Lines must be ordered chronologically by the date component embedded in the filename (oldest first, newest last).

   Example line (do not literally copy):  
   config_20230115.conf<TAB>17

4. File: /home/user/output/all_keys.txt  
   Format requirements: one key per line, all lower-case, **sorted alphabetically (LC-collate), no duplicates**.  
   The list must represent the union of keys found in every configuration file (again ignoring comments and blank lines).  
   Use Unix LF line endings.

5. Directory: /home/user/clean_configs/ containing one sanitised copy of *every* original configuration file.  
   Sanitisation rules for each line:  
       • Remove leading and trailing whitespace from both key and value.  
       • Convert the key to lower-case (value is left untouched).  
       • Discard comment lines and blank lines entirely.  
   File names must be identical to the originals, e.g. /home/user/clean_configs/config_20230115.conf.  
   The order of lines inside each sanitised file must be the same as their appearance in the corresponding original after comment/blank-line removal.

6. File: /home/user/output/new_keys.log  
   Purpose: show which keys exist in the *newest* configuration file but were **absent** from the *oldest* configuration file.  
   The newest file is the one whose date suffix is latest; the oldest is the one whose date suffix is earliest.  
   Format requirements:  
       • One key per line, lower-case, sorted alphabetically.  
       • No header, no additional spacing, Unix LF endings.

Hints (NOT mandatory):  
 • You can obtain the date component with basename and parameter expansion or simple awk/sed.  
 • `grep -Ev` or awk pattern matching will help exclude comments/blank lines.  
 • To trim whitespace with sed: `s/^[[:space:]]*//;s/[[:space:]]*$//`.  
 • Use awk associative arrays to build unique-key sets.  

The automated test harness will verify:

 • Existence of both directories with any reasonable user-writable permissions.  
 • Exact byte-for-byte contents of the four files described above.  
 • That only the required files appear in /home/user/output/ (extraneous files will not fail the test but are discouraged).

Work only in the terminal; do not modify this task description.  Good luck!
truth:
Initial filesystem state BEFORE the agent starts:

1. Directory /home/user/configs/ already exists and contains exactly three files with these exact contents (all have a trailing newline):

/home/user/configs/config_20230115.conf
------------------------------------------------
# Sample app config
  host = localhost
port=8080
max_connections = 100
# End

/home/user/configs/config_20230410.conf
------------------------------------------------
# Updated config
host = prod.example.com
port = 80
max_connections= 250
timeout = 30

/home/user/configs/config_20230718.conf
------------------------------------------------
## Production patch
host=prod.example.com
port=443
max_connections =300
timeout=45
enable_tls = true
#some comment
  cache_size= 512


EXPECTED final artefacts (exact contents, each line ends with Unix LF):

/home/user/output/config_line_counts.tsv
------------------------------------------------
config_20230115.conf\t3\n
config_20230410.conf\t4\n
config_20230718.conf\t6\n

/home/user/output/all_keys.txt
------------------------------------------------
cache_size\n
enable_tls\n
host\n
max_connections\n
port\n
timeout\n

Sanitised files:

/home/user/clean_configs/config_20230115.conf
------------------------------------------------
host=localhost\n
port=8080\n
max_connections=100\n

/home/user/clean_configs/config_20230410.conf
------------------------------------------------
host=prod.example.com\n
port=80\n
max_connections=250\n
timeout=30\n

/home/user/clean_configs/config_20230718.conf
------------------------------------------------
host=prod.example.com\n
port=443\n
max_connections=300\n
timeout=45\n
enable_tls=true\n
cache_size=512\n

/home/user/output/new_keys.log
------------------------------------------------
cache_size\n
enable_tls\n
timeout\n

The directories /home/user/output/ and /home/user/clean_configs/ must both exist; mode 755 is acceptable.

No other ground-truth requirements.
